segments.inferno-23-g75: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this clause to what precedes. un che introduces an indefinite person ('one who' / 'someone who') followed by ’ntese, an archaic/poetic perfect tense meaning 'heard' (past action). la parola tosca is the direct object: la parola = 'the word/speech' and tosca is an adjective meaning 'harsh' or 'rough' (poetic).
segments.inferno-23-g76: di retro a noi is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from behind us' (di = 'from', retro = 'behind', a noi = 'to/at us' but here idiomatically 'behind us'). gridò is the passato remoto of gridare ('he/she/it shouted'), placed at the end as the main verb of the clause.
segments.inferno-23-g77: Tenete is the 2nd person plural imperative (or present indicative used as a command) of tenere: here an instruction 'hold' or 'stay'. i piedi is the direct object 'the feet' (literally 'hold your feet', i.e. 'stand fast' / 'stay where you are').
segments.inferno-23-g78: voi che correte is a vocative + relative clause: voi 'you' addressed, che correte 'who run' (present indicative, describing those being addressed). sì is an adverb intensifying 'so' / 'thus'. per l’aura fosca = 'through the dark air/breeze' (per = 'through', l’ aura = 'the air/breeze', fosca = 'dark/gloomy'), describing how they run.
segments.inferno-23-g79: Forse is an adverb 'perhaps' introducing uncertainty. ch’ is the clitic form of che ('that'); avrai is future indicative 2nd person singular of avere 'you will have'. da me means 'from me'. quel che tu chiedi is a relative clause: quel = 'that' / 'what', che = 'which/that', tu chiedi = 'you ask' (present). The whole is a polite promise: 'Perhaps you will obtain from me that which you ask.'
segments.inferno-23-g80: Onde here functions as a discourse connector 'whereupon' / 'therefore' (not a question). ’l duca = 'the duke/leader' (Virgil); si volse is the reflexive passato remoto of volgere 'to turn' ('he turned himself' = 'he turned around'). e disse 'and said' introduces his speech.
segments.inferno-23-g81: Aspetta is the 2nd person singular (or polite imperative) of aspettare: here an imperative 'Wait.' It's a direct command addressed to Dante/the narrator or to those moving.
segments.inferno-23-g82: e poi 'and then' links the sequence; secondo il suo passo is a prepositional phrase 'according to his pace' (secondo = 'according to', il suo passo = 'his pace/step'). procedi is the 2nd person singular imperative of procedere: 'proceed' or 'follow' (i.e. keep step with him).
segments.inferno-23-g83: Ristetti is the 1st person singular passato remoto of ristare/ristare's variant: 'I stopped' / 'I stayed back' — a short, narrative past tense indicating Dante paused.
segments.inferno-23-g84: e vidi due mostrar is a construction with vidi (I saw) followed by an infinitive (mostrar = 'to show'), meaning 'I saw two (people) show...'. gran fretta 'great haste' is the object of mostrar; de l’animo literally 'of the spirit' (i.e. eagerness of heart/mind). In older Italian, infinitives after verbs of perception are common.
segments.inferno-23-g85: col is the contraction of con + il 'with the'; viso here means 'face' or 'expression' (i.e. 'with their faces / in their expression'), indicating how they showed their haste.
segments.inferno-23-g86: d’esser is a contraction of di + esser (infinitive 'to be') introducing the content of their expression: 'to be with me' (meco = 'with me'). The infinitive here expresses their desire/intent: they appeared eager to be with the speaker.
segments.inferno-23-g87: ma 'but' contrasts the previous eagerness. tardavali is an archaic/poetic imperfect or past form meaning 'they were delayed' / 'they lagged' (constructed from tardare with enclitic or older endings). ’l carco e la via stretta = 'the burden/load and the narrow way' are the causes that held them back (carco = load/burden, via stretta = narrow path).
segments.inferno-23-g103: Ma is a simple adversative conjunction ('but') introducing the question; voi chi siete is an interrogative sequence where chi (who) functions as predicate nominative and siete is the present indicative, 2nd-person plural of essere — the normal word order here places voi (you) before chi to emphasize the addressee.
segments.inferno-23-g104: a cui is a preposition + relative pronoun ('to/for whom' in the older/poetic use here giving the sense 'from whom'); tanto distilla is a present indicative verb distillare used impersonally of tears (‘so much drips’), with tanto 'so much' as its quantity modifier; quant' i' veggio is an elliptical comparative 'as I see' where quant' = quanto and i' is the poetically contracted io (I) before the verb veggio ('I see'); giù per le guance ('down along the cheeks') gives the path/locative ('down' + preposition per + noun phrase le guance).
segments.inferno-23-g105: e is the coordinating 'and' linking this question to the previous; che pena here is an exclamatory/interrogative use of che meaning 'what' and pena is best read as a punitive/suffering noun ('punishment' or 'penalty') — è is the present indicative 3rd-person singular of essere ('is'); in voi marks the person bearing that punishment; the second che is the relative 'that' introducing the subordinate clause; sì is the adverb 'so' or 'thus'; sfavilla is a 3rd-person singular present indicative of the verb sfavillare ('to flash/sparkle/blaze'), describing the way the punishment appears.
segments.inferno-23-g106: This segment contains only the sentence-final punctuation (a period) marking a pause in the dialogue; there are no lexical items to analyse.
segments.inferno-23-g107: E is the coordinating 'and'; l’un is the contracted form of 'lo uno' ('one of them' or 'one'), used here as the indefinite/specific subject; rispuose is the archaic past-tense form (3rd-person singular passato remoto) of rispondere ('answered'); a me marks the indirect object 'to me'.
segments.inferno-23-g108: Le cappe rance is the noun phrase 'the tawny cloaks' (cappe = cloaks/hoods; rance = tawny/orange-coloured — adjective post‑ or pre‑positonal by poetic use); son is the contracted poetic form of sono ('are'); di piombo literally 'of lead' describes their material/weight; sì grosse uses the adverb sì 'so' + grosse (here meaning large/heavy/thick) to indicate great heaviness.
segments.inferno-23-g109: che is the conjunction 'that' introducing the result clause; li pesi ('the weights') is the subject (li = the masculine plural article, pesi = weights) — here used in the sense of heavy weights attached to the cloaks; fan is the contracted form of fanno (3rd-person plural present of fare, 'make'/'cause'); così cigolar is the infinitive phrase 'to creak thus' (così 'thus/so' + cigolar 'to creak'); le lor bilance ('their balances/scales') is the object phrase, with lor = their and bilance = scales/balances.
segments.inferno-23-g29: Pur mo is an adverbial phrase (pur = even/still; mo = now/just now) giving temporal emphasis. Venieno is an archaic 3rd-person plural past/imperf. form of venire ('they came' / 'were coming') agreeing with the plural subject i tuo' pensier; the article i + possessive tuo' follows older word order (i tuo’ pensier = 'your thoughts') and tra means 'among/amongst'.
segments.inferno-23-g30: Two parallel prepositional phrases (con simile atto e con simile faccia) emphasize similarity; simile modifies both atto and faccia. Atto here means a physical gesture or movement; faccia refers to facial expression or countenance.
segments.inferno-23-g31: Sì che introduces a result clause ('so that'). D’intrambi = 'from/between the two' (di + intrambi), un sol consiglio fei uses the archaic past 1st-person fei (feci) meaning 'I made' or 'I formed' — sol = only/one functioning as an adjective before consiglio.
segments.inferno-23-g32: S’elli è che is a somewhat archaic conditional introducer ('if it be that' / 'if it is that'); elli here is an older impersonal/subject form. Giaccia is the subjunctive/archaic form of giacere 'to lie, to be situated' describing how the destra (the right side/slope) lies or rests.
segments.inferno-23-g33: This clause states the consequence that would follow: che noi possiam ne l’altra bolgia scendere. Possiam is a present subjunctive (or potential) meaning 'we may/could', ne here is the pronoun meaning 'into it/into that' attached before the article (ne + l’ = into the), and scendere is the infinitive 'to descend'.
segments.inferno-23-g34: The main clause of result: noi fuggirem is an archaic 1st-person-plural form (conditional/future sense: 'we would flee / we would escape') and takes as object l’imaginata caccia ('the imagined chase' or planned pursuit).
segments.inferno-23-g35: Già non compié di tal consiglio rendere uses archaic word order: compié is a past tense (perfect) of compiere 'to complete' in the 1st person (I had completed), but with non + già the sense is 'had not yet completed' or 'not yet finished' giving the background for the next event; rendere here means 'to deliver / to give' (i.e., to give that counsel).
segments.inferno-23-g36: Ch’io li vidi venir is a temporal/result clause introduced by che ('when/that'); vidi is the perfect past 'I saw', li is the direct-object clitic 'them', and venir is the infinitive 'to come' (here understood as 'coming'). Con l’ ali tese = 'with the wings outstretched' (ali = wings; tese = stretched). Non molto lungi = 'not very far off'.
segments.inferno-23-g37: Per introduces purpose ('in order to' / 'for the purpose of'); volerne is the infinitive volere + clitic ne ('to want/aim at some of them' or 'to take/seize him/them') where ne refers back to the object, and prendere is the infinitive 'to take, seize'. The whole phrase indicates the purpose of their coming.
segments.inferno-23-g38: Lo duca mio is 'my guide' with the older article lo before a title/noun; di sùbito = 'suddenly / immediately'. Mi prese is passato remoto (or perfect) 3rd-person singular of prendere meaning 'he seized me' (the guide is subject).
segments.inferno-23-g39: Simile simile clause compares the guide's action to a mother: ch’al romore è desta = 'who is awakened by the noise' (al romore = 'at the noise'); vede presso a sé le fiamme accese = 'sees the flames lit close to her' (presso a sé = near herself). The relative clause uses present tense for vividness.
segments.inferno-23-g40: That relative clause continues: prende il figlio e fugge e non s’arresta = 'she takes the child and flees and does not stop' — s’arresta is the reflexive form meaning 'stops herself' (i.e., does not pause).
segments.inferno-23-g41: A gerundial clause avendo più di lui che di sé cura = 'having more care for him than for herself' where avendo is a gerund ('having'), and the comparative structure più ... che ... indicates she cares more for the child (lui) than for herself (sé).
segments.inferno-23-g42: Tanto che introduces the result: 'so that' (tanto che) she is left with only one shirt. Here vesta is an archaic verbal form meaning 'she is clothed/wears' (older subjunctive/indicative form); camiscia is 'shirt/shift' — the clause expresses consequence (so that she has only a single shirt).
segments.inferno-23-g43: E giù dal collo de la ripa dura supin si diede a la pendente roccia describes the motion: 'and down from the neck of the hard bank she threw herself supine onto the sloping rock.' Supin (supine) qualifies the posture; si diede a is a reflexive idiom 'gave herself to' = 'threw/let herself' (diede = past of dare).
segments.inferno-23-g44: Che l’un de’ lati a l’altra bolgia tura is a relative clause explaining the effect of the rock: tura is an archaic verb meaning 'blocks / closes' (it closes one side toward the other bolgia), so the rock 'shuts off' one of the sides between the bolge.
segments.inferno-23-g1: Taciti is a plural adjective (masculine) placed before the (implied) subject, meaning 'silent' or 'quiet'. In Dante it functions as a quick characterizing word about the speakers (we), and its fronting is a poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-23-g2: Soli is also a plural adjective agreeing with the (implied) first‑person plural subject: 'alone' or 'by ourselves'. Here it reinforces taciti and describes the state of the speakers.
segments.inferno-23-g3: Sanza compagnia = 'without company' (sanza = archaic form of senza). N’andavam is n' + andavam (imperfect indicative, first‑person plural of andare): 'we were going / we went' — the clitic n' (ne) refers back to the lack of company (ne = 'away/from that'), a common archaic/pronominal usage. L’un dinanzi e l’altro dopo literally lists the relative positions: l' (elided article) + un/altro = 'one'/'the other'; dinanzi = 'in front'; dopo = 'behind/after'. Word order is normal for a poetic enumeration.
segments.inferno-23-g4: Come introduces a simile ('like/as'). Frati minor = 'Friars Minor' (the name of the mendicant order; minor here is postpositive and identifies the order). Vanno is present indicative third plural 'they go' used generically to describe how such friars walk; per via = 'along the road' or 'by the way' (per = along/through, via = road/way).
segments.inferno-23-g5: Vòlt' era in su la favola d’Isopo is an idiomatic, slightly archaic expression: vòlt' (volto) + era (imperfect of essere) yields 'had turned' or 'was turned' — i.e. 'my thought had turned to'. In su here collocates with vòlt' era to mean 'turned upon/into'. Lo mio pensier is the subject ('my thought'); per la presente rissa = 'because of/on account of the present quarrel' (per = because of/for). Note the older word order and the elided forms (Vòlt' for volto, lo for the article before mio).
segments.inferno-23-g6: Dov' introduces a relative/subordinate clause meaning 'where' or 'in which'. El parlò is parlò (passato remoto, third singular) with el = 'he' (referring to Aesop); 'parlò' is the narrative past 'spoke/related'. De (archaic di) introduces the objects of the fable: la rana e del topo = 'the frog and the mouse' (del = di + il).
segments.inferno-23-g7: Ché = 'for/because'. Più non si pareggia = 'no longer is compared / no longer matches' — si is the impersonal/reciprocal clitic and pareggia is present indicative third singular of pareggiare. 'Mo' e 'issa' are dialect/colloquial words (mo = now, issa = that/this in dialect) presented as quoted forms; che introduces a relative clause: 'which (one) with the other does' — l'un con l'altro fa = 'one does (to) the other' or 'what one makes with the other'. Fa is present indicative 'does/makes'.
segments.inferno-23-g8: Se ben s’accoppia = 'if indeed / even if ... are joined'. Se ben is a concessive/conditional phrase ('if even' / 'if indeed'); s'accoppia is reflexive (si accoppia) in the third singular 'is joined / are coupled'. Principio e fine = 'beginning and end'; con la mente fissa = 'with the mind fixed' (mente is feminine, so fissa agrees), i.e. 'with a fixed purpose/intent'. Grammatically this is a conditional subordinate ('if... [they] are coupled with a fixed mind').
segments.inferno-23-g9: E come = 'And as/just as' introducing a parallel action. L’un pensier de l’altro = 'one thought of the other' (l'un = 'one', pensier = 'thought', de = of, l'altro = 'the other'); the word order reflects Dante's poetic compression. Scoppia is present indicative 'bursts/explodes' — used to describe a sudden mental reaction.
segments.inferno-23-g10: Così nacque = 'thus/so was born' (nacque is passato remoto, third singular of nascere — 'sprang up' or 'arose'). Di quello = 'from that (one)'; un altro poi = 'another afterwards' (poi = then/afterwards). The sequence presents a chain of thoughts: one bursting forth causes another to arise.
segments.inferno-23-g11: Che introduces the result/clause: 'so that'. La prima paura = 'the first fear' (definite article + adjective + noun). Mi fé = mi (to me) + fé (archaic/poetic spelling of fece, passato remoto of fare) — 'made (to) me' or 'caused me to be'. Doppia is an adjective agreeing with paura: 'double' or 'twice as much'.
segments.inferno-23-g12: Io pensava così — Io is the subject 'I'; pensava is the imperfect indicative first singular ('I was thinking' or 'I thought'); così = 'thus' or 'like this'. The imperfect marks an ongoing mental process that introduces the reported thought.
segments.inferno-23-g13: Questi per noi sono scherniti = 'These (people) are mocked in relation to us' (per noi can mean 'in our regard' or 'to our detriment'); sono scherniti is a present passive construction 'are mocked/derided' (scherniti = past participle). Con danno e con beffa = 'with damage/harm and with mockery' (beffa = taunt/mockery); sì fatta = 'so made/so contrived' — an adjectival phrase qualifying the mockery/action (sì intensifies fatta).
segments.inferno-23-g14: Ch' assai credo che lor nòi — ch' (= che) introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; assai modifies credo = 'I very much believe' or 'I strongly think' (credo is present indicative 'I believe'). Lor is the indirect object 'to them'. Nòi (an archaic/dialectal form spelled with stress) is given here as 'noi' ('we/us') in the word list, but the exact sense and syntactic function of this short phrase is elliptical in the text (Dante is introducing reported thought and the phrase is dialectal/elliptical). Grammatically it is a complement after credo; scholars note the phrase is terse/colloquial.
segments.inferno-23-g15: Se is the conditional conjunction 'if'. l' ira is the subject 'the anger' (l' = il). sovra is a preposition 'over/on' and ’l mal voler = 'il mal voler' = 'the ill-will' (mal + voler = bad will/ill intention). s’aggueffa is a reflexive verb (s’ = si) meaning 'fastens on / lays hold of'; it is a present-form used here in a general/hypothetical sense ('if anger fastens on...').
segments.inferno-23-g16: ei is the subject pronoun 'they' (the devils). ne is a clitic particle here referring back to 'us' or 'upon us' (partitive/relational use with venire). verranno is the future of venire, 'they will come'; dietro is an adverb 'after/behind'. più crudeli is the comparative 'more cruel' agreeing with ei. che introduces a comparison 'than'; the remainder (’l cane a quella lievre ch’elli acceffa’) forms the standard comparative clause: 'the dog toward that hare which he seizes/has seized.'
segments.inferno-23-g17: Già is an adverb 'already'. mi sentia is the imperfect 'I felt' (sentire in the imperfect, reflexive construction 'mi sentia [infinitive]'). tutti arricciar li peli is a catenative structure: 'tutti' ('all/completely') + infinitive arricciar 'to bristle/ curl' with li peli 'the hairs' as the object (so: 'I felt all the hairs bristle'). de la paura = 'from/of fear'. e stava in dietro intento: stava is the imperfect of stare 'I stood/was', in dietro 'at the back', intento 'attentive/intent'.
segments.inferno-23-g18: quand' is the clitic/elided form of quando 'when'. io dissi uses the stressed pronoun io + dissi, passato remoto of dire, 'I said' (narrative past). The colon leads into direct speech.
segments.inferno-23-g19: Maestro is a vocative noun 'Master' (address to his guide). The form is a straightforward direct-address noun.
segments.inferno-23-g20: se non celi te e me tostamente is a conditional protasis 'if you do not hide you and me quickly'. se = 'if'. non negates. celi here stands for a verbal form 'celare' (to hide/conceal) in the relevant mood (older/poetic subjunctive/imperative-shaded form used in conditional clauses). te e me are the two objects 'you and me' clitic order is disjoint reflexive/strong pronouns. tostamente is an adverb 'quickly/soon'.
segments.inferno-23-g21: i' ho pavento = 'I have fear' (i' is the contracted io, ho is present of avere, pavento = fear/dread) — a common way to say 'I am afraid'. d’i Malebranche = 'of the Malebranche' (di + article elided), the name of the devils.
segments.inferno-23-g22: Noi is the subject 'we'. li is the clitic direct object 'them' (referring to the Malebranche) and appears before the verb. avem is an archaic present form 'abbiamo' (we have). già 'already'. dietro 'behind' (implying 'behind us').
segments.inferno-23-g23: io li ’magino sì — io 'I' (subject), li 'them' (object clitic), ’magino is the verb 'imagino' (I imagine/suppose) written with an apostrophe, and sì 'so/thus' modifies the manner: 'I imagine them so/like that.' The clitic li again precedes the verb.
segments.inferno-23-g24: che introduces a result/cause clause 'so that/that'; già 'already'. li (again the object clitic 'them') and sento 'I hear/sense' (present of sentire). The whole clause means 'so that I already hear them.'
segments.inferno-23-g25: E is the conjunction 'and'. quei is an archaic demonstrative/pronoun 'that one' used here as 'he/that one' introducing the speaker of the next speech; it functions like 'he (the devil) replied.'
segments.inferno-23-g26: S’i’ fossi di piombato vetro is a counterfactual clause: S’i’ is the contracted form of 'Se io' ('If I'); fossi is the imperfect subjunctive of essere 'were'; di piombato vetro is an adjectival phrase 'of leaded glass' ('piombato' = leaded/leaden, vetro = glass) describing a hypothetical material condition.
segments.inferno-23-g27: l’imagine di fuor tua non trarrei più tosto a me — l' imagine 'the image' (l' = the); di fuor tua = 'of/outside your' = 'your outward image' (fuor = outside/outward); non trarrei is the first-person conditional 'I would not draw'; più tosto = 'sooner/rather' (combined comparative adverb); a me = 'to me'. The conditional trarrei depends on the counterfactual in the previous segment.
segments.inferno-23-g28: che introduces the comparison/relative 'than/which'; quella dentro = 'that one within' (the inner image). ’mpetro is an elided form of impetro (from Latin impetrare) meaning 'I obtain/get' (first-person present). So the clause reads 'than that which I obtain from within.'
segments.inferno-23-g110: 'Frati godenti fummo' places the noun phrase (frati godenti) before the verb fummo (passato remoto of essere) for poetic emphasis; godenti is a present participle used adjectivally ('pleasure‑loving'/'enjoying') modifying frati, and fummo is the main verb 'we were'.
segments.inferno-23-g111: The conjunction e ('and') links a second predicate; bolognesi is a predicate adjective in the nominative plural agreeing with the (implicit) subject 'we' established by fummo, so 'and (we were) Bolognese.'
segments.inferno-23-g112: This is an appositive/naming clause: the pronoun io ('I') is fronted for emphasis, followed by the name Catalano; e questi ('and this one') introduces the companion Loderingo. Nomati is a past participle used predicatively ('named') applying to the two persons — the copula is left understood from the previous fummo.
segments.inferno-23-g113: e ('and') continues the sentence; da tua terra is a prepositional phrase 'from your land/town' (tua refers to the addressee's place). Insieme presi uses presi (past participle/past form of prendere) elliptically with the earlier copula ('(we) taken together'), and the following simile come suole esser tolto un uom solingo = 'as one is wont to be taken away a solitary man' where suole is the habitual present 'is wont' and esser tolto is a passive infinitive.
segments.inferno-23-g114: per + infinitive (per conservar) marks purpose: 'in order to preserve.' Sua is a third‑person possessive referring back to the solitary man (his), and pace is the direct object 'peace' or 'quiet.'
segments.inferno-23-g115: e fummo tali: e ('and') connects; fummo is again the passato remoto 'we were,' and tali is a predicative adjective 'such' (plural) referring back to the kind of friars just described — 'and we were such.'
segments.inferno-23-g116: ch' is the contraction of che introducing a result/relative clause ('that'); ancor = ancora 'still/yet'; si here is the impersonal/reflexive si used with parere to mean 'one/it seems'; pare = 'seems/appears'; intorno dal Gardingo = 'around/by the Gardingo' (dal = da + il). Together: 'so that even now one still appears around the Gardingo.' Gardingo is a proper name.
segments.inferno-23-g117: Io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'; cominciai is passato remoto 'I began' (narrative past), and the colon introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-23-g118: O is the vocative interjection 'O' (addressing someone); frati ('brothers'/'friars') is a vocative plural noun directly addressed by the speaker.
segments.inferno-23-g119: i vostri mali is a noun phrase 'the/your troubles/evils' (i = definite article; vostri = your, plural possessive; mali = plural of male meaning 'ills/evils/troubles'). The sentence trails off (see following punctuation) rather than completing the clause.
segments.inferno-23-g120: A single period/point in the printed text marking a sharp stop or interruption in the speech; grammatically it signals the truncation of the utterance.
segments.inferno-23-g121: Another isolated punctuation mark (a period), continuing the printed interruption or pause; it carries no lexical content.
segments.inferno-23-g157: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' introducing the speaker; 'l' is the elided article ('il') before a noun; 'frate' is the nominal subject meaning 'friar' (here the speaker). The colon marks direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-23-g158: Io is the first-person subject 'I'. Udi' is the past form (poetic contraction of 'udii' or 'udire' in past) meaning 'I heard'. Già means 'already' (indicating prior knowledge). Dire here is the infinitive used with 'udire' to mean 'hear say' or 'hear people say'. 'A Bologna' uses the preposition a + place name, idiomatically 'in Bologna' (a can mark location). 'Del diavol' is the contraction of di + il = 'of the' forming 'of the devil'; 'vizi assai' = 'vices very/many' where assai modifies vizi.
segments.inferno-23-g159: Tra quali = 'among which' (tra = among; quali = which/those which, referring back to 'vizi'). Udi' again is 'I heard' (repeated reporting verb). Ch' is the clitic form of 'che' (= 'that'). 'Elli' is an archaic pronoun here referring to the devil (used where Modern Italian would have 'egli' or 'egli/esso'); it functions as the subject. È is the present tense of 'essere' ('is'). Bugiardo is a predicative adjective/noun 'liar' or 'deceitful'.
segments.inferno-23-g160: E = 'and' linking the two appositive descriptions. Padre is a noun 'father' used figuratively. Di = the preposition 'of'. Menzogna is a noun 'lie/falsehood', completing the fixed expression 'father of lies'. The segment closes the friar's direct speech.
segments.inferno-23-g161: Appresso is an adverb meaning 'afterward' or 'next'. 'Il duca' is the noun phrase 'the leader/duke' (here Virgil as guide). 'A gran passi' is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'with great strides' (a + adjective + plural noun). 'Sen gì' is a contracted past form (sen gí = se n' and gí) meaning 'went away'—gì is past 'went'.
segments.inferno-23-g162: Turbato is a past participle used adjectivally 'disturbed' (describing the duca). Un poco = 'a little'. D'ira = contraction of 'di ira' = 'by/with anger' (expressing cause or source). Nel sembiante = 'in the countenance', i.e. 'in his expression/face'; nel = in + il.
segments.inferno-23-g163: Ond' is the contracted form of 'onde' (here 'whereupon'/'therefore') linking result or consequence. Io is 'I'. Da li ’ncarcati = 'from those charged/burdened' (da = from; li = the; 'ncarcati is an archaic/spelled variant meaning 'incaricati' or 'charged/loaded'). Mi parti' is the reflexive past action 'I parted/left' (mi = myself; parti' = past 'partii/partì' in archaic spelling). Dietro a le poste = 'behind the posts' (dietro = behind; a + definite article + poste), de le care piante = 'of the dear plants' (de = of; le care piante = the beloved plants/trees) completing the prepositional phrase.
segments.inferno-23-g60: Di fuor dorate son is an inverted predicative phrase: Di fuor ('on the outside') is a prepositional phrase placed before the adjective dorate ('gilded') and the verb son (archaic/poetic for sono, 'are'), yielding 'they are gilded on the outside.' The word order (adjective before verb) is typical of poetic compression.
segments.inferno-23-g61: sì ch’elli abbaglia is a result clause: sì che (contracted to sì ch’) = 'so that,' elli = 'they' (subject), and abbaglia is present indicative 'dazzle/blind.' In Dante's usage verb forms and pronouns sometimes show archaic or flexible agreement, but functionally this reads 'so that they dazzle.'
segments.inferno-23-g62: ma dentro tutte piombo is elliptical: ma dentro = 'but inside,' tutte (feminine plural) agrees with the implied things (the cloaks) and piombo is a predicative noun 'lead.' The copula 'sono' is omitted: '[but] inside they are all lead.'
segments.inferno-23-g63: e gravi tanto continues the predicate: e = 'and,' gravi is the adjective 'heavy' (plural agreeing with the cloaks), and tanto is an adverbial intensifier 'so much/so heavy.'
segments.inferno-23-g64: che introduces the result clause 'that Frederick used to pad them with straw.' Federigo (here the historical Frederick II) is the subject; le is the object clitic referring back to the cloaks ('them'); mettea is the imperfect of mettere (archaic spelling) expressing habitual or repeated past action ('used to put'); di paglia indicates material 'with straw.'
segments.inferno-23-g65: This is an exclamatory nominal phrase. Oh/O is the vocative/exclamation; in + etterno is a prepositional phrase with the archaic spelling etterno = 'eternal'; faticoso qualifies the noun manto ('mantle, cloak'), so the whole is an outcry about the 'eternal, laborious cloak.'
segments.inferno-23-g66: Noi ci volgemmo is a reflexive perfective action (volgemmo = passato remoto of volgere, 'we turned ourselves'); ancor pur = 'again/still'; a man manca is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic locution meaning 'to the left' (literally 'to the left hand'); con loro insieme = 'together with them' (redundant togetherness for emphasis).
segments.inferno-23-g67: intenti al tristo pianto: intenti (predicate adjective, plural) = 'attentive/ intent,' and prepositional phrase al tristo pianto = 'to the sad lament' (intento a + noun = 'attentive to').
segments.inferno-23-g68: ma per lo peso indicates cause 'but because of the weight/burden' (per = 'because of/through'); quella gente stanca = 'that/those people, tired' (stanca = adjective 'weary'); venìa is the imperfect 'were coming/advanced' (continuous or repeated action), sì pian = 'so slowly.'
segments.inferno-23-g69: che introduces a result clause: 'that we were new ...' noi eravam (eravam = imperfect 'we were'); nuovi here is figurative 'new/unused' meaning inexperienced or unfamiliar; di compagnia ad ogne mover d’anca = 'in accompanying (their) every movement of the hip' — compagnia + ad expresses being in the company of/used to accompany; mover d'anca = 'hip-movement,' describing the slow gait.
segments.inferno-23-g70: Per ch’io is a discourse connector meaning 'so/therefore I' (per che/io = 'so that I'); al duca mio = indirect object 'to my duke/guide' (duca here = 'guide' referring to Virgil). The colon marks the beginning of direct speech.
segments.inferno-23-g71: Fa che tu trovi is an imperative construction: Fa (imperative 'make'/'do') + che + subjunctive (trovi) expresses an order: 'make that you find' = 'see to it that you find.' alcun = 'someone'; ch’al fatto o al nome si conosca is a relative clause where si conosca is present subjunctive passive/reflexive ('be known'/'be recognized') — 'known by deed or by name.'
segments.inferno-23-g72: e li occhi = 'and the eyes' (introducing the next command about the eyes); li is the article here marking occhi, functioning as the direct object of the upcoming verb.
segments.inferno-23-g73: sì andando is a circumstantial gerund phrase: sì = 'as/while' and andando is the gerund 'going' — 'as you go' or 'while proceeding.'
segments.inferno-23-g74: intorno movi is the imperative instruction 'move (them) around' — intorno = 'around,' and movi is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person singular imperative of muovere ('move/turn'), meaning 'turn your eyes about.'
segments.inferno-23-g45: Non corse mai: 'corse' is passato remoto (3rd sg. of correre) meaning 'ran/flowed'; with 'non ... mai' it yields 'never ran'. 'sì tosto' is an adverbial phrase 'so/so quickly'. 'acqua' is the subject. 'per doccia' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'through a doccia' (a channel/sluice for the mill). 'a volger' uses the preposition 'a' + infinitive to indicate purpose ('to turn'). The noun phrase 'ruota di molin terragno' = 'wheel of a ground/land mill', with 'di' marking possession and 'terragno' qualifying the mill.
segments.inferno-23-g46: quand' ella più verso le pale approccia: 'quand'' is the contracted form of 'quando' ('when'); 'ella' (referring back to 'acqua') = 'it'; 'più verso le pale' = 'closer toward the blades' and is an adverbial phrase placed before the verb; 'approccia' is present indicative (3rd sg. of approcciare) meaning 'approaches'.
segments.inferno-23-g47: come 'l maestro mio per quel vivagno: 'come' introduces the comparison 'as'; ''l maestro mio' = 'my master' (definite article + noun + possessive). 'per quel vivagno' means 'over/along that vivagno' — 'vivagno' is an embankment/bank; 'per' here indicates movement along/over that bank.
segments.inferno-23-g48: portandosene me sovra 'l suo petto: 'portandosene' is a gerund/participle form built from portare + si + ne (portando + si + ne), literally 'carrying himself away (with it/them)' and here idiomatically 'carrying me away with him'; the separate object pronoun 'me' is given for emphasis/clarity. 'sovra 'l suo petto' = 'upon his chest' (sovra = on/upon, 'l = the, suo petto = his chest).
segments.inferno-23-g49: come suo figlio,: simple simile 'as his son'; 'suo' is the possessive agreeing with 'figlio'. The comma marks the pause before the contrasting clause that follows.
segments.inferno-23-g50: non come compagno.: contrastive negation 'not as a companion.' 'non' negates the comparison; 'come compagno' = 'as (a) companion', with compagno the noun in apposition.
segments.inferno-23-g51: A pena fuoro i piè suoi giunti al letto del fondo giù: 'A pena' is an idiom meaning 'scarcely/hardly'. Word order is inverted: 'fuoro' (passato remoto 3rd pl. of essere) + past participle 'giunti' yields 'had reached/arrived'. 'i piè suoi' = 'their feet' (possesive after noun), 'al letto del fondo giù' = 'to the bed of the bottom down below' (adverb 'giù' = 'down').
segments.inferno-23-g52: ch'e' furon in sul colle sovresso noi;: 'ch'' is a contraction of 'che' and introduces the clause ('when/that'); 'e' here belongs to the verb cluster (older orthography) and 'furon' is passato remoto 'they were' (furono). 'in sul colle' = 'on the hill', 'sovresso noi' = 'over/above us' (sovresso = above/upon, placed after the noun).
segments.inferno-23-g53: ma non lì era sospetto:: 'ma' = 'but'; 'non lì era' = 'there was no' (imperfect 'era' = 'was'); 'sospetto' is a noun meaning 'suspicion/fear'. The clause states that at that spot there was no apprehension.
segments.inferno-23-g54: ché l'alta provedenza che lor volle porre ministri de la fossa quinta,: 'ché' = 'for/because'. 'l'alta provedenza' = 'the high/lofty Providence' (adjective before noun). 'che lor volle porre ministri' is a relative clause: 'which willed to place them ministers' — 'volle' is passato remoto of volere; 'porre' is the infinitive 'to place'. 'de la fossa quinta' = 'of the fifth ditch/pit'.
segments.inferno-23-g55: poder di partirs' indi a tutti tolle.: main verb here is 'tolle' (from togliere, present/archaic form) meaning 'takes away'. 'poder di partirs'' = 'the power/ability to depart oneself' (partirs' = partirsi, 'to leave'), 'indi' = 'thence', 'a tutti' = 'from all' (lit. 'to all' but functionally 'from everyone'). The clause means 'takes away from everyone the power to depart thence.'
segments.inferno-23-g56: Là giù trovammo una gente dipinta che giva intorno assai con lenti passi,: 'Là giù' = 'down there' (two adverbs together for emphasis). 'trovammo' is passato remoto (we found). 'una gente dipinta' = 'a people painted' (dipinta is a past participle used adjectivally). 'che giva' is a relative clause; 'giva' is the imperfect (archaic form of 'andava') 3rd sg. 'it/the people went about' — note 'gente' is a collective noun treated as singular, hence singular verb. 'intorno assai' = 'around a lot', 'con lenti passi' = 'with slow steps'.
segments.inferno-23-g57: piangendo e nel sembiante stanca e vinta.: 'piangendo' is a gerund/participle 'weeping'; 'e' = 'and'. 'nel sembiante' = 'in the countenance/appearance' (i.e. in their faces). 'stanca e vinta' are adjectives agreeing with 'gente' (feminine singular in form) meaning 'tired and conquered/overcome'.
segments.inferno-23-g58: Elli avean cappe con cappucci bassi davanti a li occhi,: 'Elli' is an archaic form of 'essi' meaning 'they'. 'avean' is the imperfect (they had). 'cappe' = 'capes/cloaks'. 'con cappucci bassi' = 'with low hoods' and 'davanzi a li occhi' = 'in front of their eyes' (i.e. hood drawn low over the eyes). Word order is typical: verb after subject pronoun and object, and adjectives follow the nouns they modify.
segments.inferno-23-g59: fatte de la taglia che in Clugnì per li monaci fassi.: 'fatte' (past participle feminine plural) agrees with 'cappe' and means 'made'. 'de la taglia' = 'of the cut/style'. The relative clause 'che in Clugnì per li monaci fassi' uses 'fassi' (an archaic subjunctive/passive sense of 'fare') to mean 'that was/is made in Cluny for the monks' — a statement about the cut being that used at Cluny for monks.
segments.inferno-23-g88: 'Quando' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'fuor' is the archaic adverb 'fuori' (outside) placed before the past participle, and 'giunti' is the past participle of 'giungere' used absolutely to mean 'having come/arrived'; together the phrase means 'when (they) had arrived outside'.
segments.inferno-23-g89: 'assai' is an adverb ('much/very') modifying the following action; 'con l'occhio bieco' is an instrumental-prepositional phrase ('with a sidelong/hostile eye') where the article 'l'' precedes 'occhio'; 'mi' is the object clitic 'me' placed before the verb; 'rimiraron' is the 3rd-person plural past (poetic) of 'rimirare' 'they looked/inspected'; 'sanza far parola' is an idiomatic adverbial construction 'without making a word' = 'without speaking'.
segments.inferno-23-g90: 'poi' = 'then'; 'si' is the reflexive/clitic marking that the subject turns itself; 'volsero' is the 3rd‑person plural past of 'volgere' ('they turned'); 'in sé' literally 'into themselves' = 'to one another' or 'inwardly' (an idiom meaning they turned their attention inward/among themselves).
segments.inferno-23-g91: 'e' = 'and'; 'dicean' is the imperfect (poetic) of 'dire', 3rd‑person plural 'they were saying' or 'they said' in an ongoing sense; 'seco' (with him / with themselves) here means 'among themselves' or 'to one another' (an old reflexive/medial form).
segments.inferno-23-g92: 'Costui' is the demonstrative 'this one/this man' used vocatively; 'par' is the poetic present of 'parere' 'seems'; 'vivo' is predicate adjective 'alive'; 'a' here marks the source/reason ('from/by'); 'l’atto' = 'the act/gesture' (atto = gesture here); 'de la gola' = 'of the throat' (gola taken literally). The whole clause means 'This one seems alive from the motion/gesture of the throat.'
segments.inferno-23-g93: 'e' = 'and'; the sequence here corresponds to the conditional 'se' ('if') plus the compound predicate: 'son' = present of 'esser' ('are'), and 'morti' is the past participle used adjectivally ('dead'); the clause reads 'and if they are dead'. (The printed clitic forms are archaic/contractional but function as 'if' + 'are'.)
segments.inferno-23-g94: 'per qual privilegio' = 'by/through what privilege' ('per' introducing the means/reason and 'qual' = 'what'); 'vanno scoperti' is a present indicative plural 'they go/are going uncovered' where 'scoperti' (past participle) functions adjectivally ('uncovered, bare'); 'de la grave stola' = 'of the grave/solemn stole' (a heavy or ceremonial robe). The question asks by what special right they are allowed to go without their solemn stole.
segments.inferno-23-g95: This segment is a sentence-final period (punctuation) marking the end of the preceding speech; there are no lexical items here to parse.
segments.inferno-23-g96: 'Poi' = 'then'; 'disser' is the (poetic) past historic plural of 'dire' ('they said'); 'me' is the indirect object 'to me' — 'then they said to me'.
segments.inferno-23-g97: 'O' is the vocative interjection 'O' (addressing someone); 'Tosco' is a vocative/noun 'Tuscan' (addressing Dante as a fellow Tuscan).
segments.inferno-23-g98: 'ch’' is the relative/introducing particle 'che' here meaning 'who'/'that'; 'al collegio' = 'to the company/association' ('a' + definite article); 'de l’ipocriti tristi' = 'of the sad/wretched hypocrites' (genitive phrase describing the company); 'se’ venuto' is an archaic 2nd-person perfect construction ('sei venuto' = 'you have come') where 'se' stands for 'sei' and 'venuto' is the past participle.
segments.inferno-23-g99: 'dir' is the infinitive 'to say/tell' used as an imperative-style request 'tell'; 'chi' = 'who'; 'tu' = 'you'; 'se’' here stands for 'sei' = 'you are' (archaic); 'non' negates; 'avere in dispregio' is an idiom 'to have (someone) in contempt' — so 'dir chi tu se' non avere in dispregio' = 'tell who you are so that you are not held in contempt' (purpose/resultive infinitive clause).
segments.inferno-23-g100: 'E' = 'and'; 'io' = 'I' (subject); 'a loro' = 'to them' (indirect object) — 'And I to them:' introduces Dante's reply.
segments.inferno-23-g101: The clause 'I' fui nato e cresciuto' uses the poetic auxiliary 'fui' with past participles to mean 'I was born and was raised' (a perfect/past sense); 'sovra 'l bel fiume d'Arno' is a locative phrase 'over/on the fair river Arno'; 'a la gran villa' is another locative 'at the great town' — overall 'I was born and brought up on the fair Arno at the great town'.
segments.inferno-23-g102: 'e son' = 'and I am' (present 'son' = 'sono'); 'col corpo' = 'with the body' (col = con + il); 'ch’i’ ho sempre avuto' is a relative clause 'that I have always had' where 'ch’' = 'che' (that), 'i’' is an old contractionstanding for 'io' (here represented in the materials as the definite article form, but functioning as the clitic 'I' in Dante's orthography) and 'ho sempre avuto' = 'I have always had'. The whole means 'and I am with the body I have always had.'
segments.inferno-23-g143: Allor is a temporal adverb ('then'). vid' is the archaic contracted past (vidi) and is followed by the subject pronoun io (inverted for emphasis): 'I saw.' maravigliar is an infinitive used after a verb of perception (vidi maravigliar...) with Virgilio as the logical subject of that infinitive ('I saw Virgil marvel'). sovra introduces the object phrase 'sovra colui...' meaning 'at/over him,' and the relative clause ch’era disteso in croce uses era (imperfect) + past participle disteso to mean 'who was stretched/laid out on the cross.' tanto vilmente is an adverbial phrase modifying how he was laid out, and ne l’etterno essilio uses the poetic ne + article to mean 'in the eternal exile.'
segments.inferno-23-g144: Poscia is a sequential adverb ('afterwards/then'). drizzò is the past historic (drizzare) meaning 'directed/raised'; al frate is the indirect object 'to the friar.' cotal voce 'such a voice' (cotal = such) indicates the manner of address.
segments.inferno-23-g145: Non vi dispiaccia is a polite formula using the present subjunctive dispiaccia ('may it not displease') with vi as the indirect object 'to you' — literally, 'do not be displeased' or 'please don't mind.'
segments.inferno-23-g146: se vi lece is an idiomatic conditional request: se = 'if,' vi = 'to you,' and lece (archaic) = 'it is permitted/fits' (similar to 'se vi piace' or 'if you allow it'), i.e. 'if it pleases you / if you will allow.'
segments.inferno-23-g147: dirci is the infinitive + clitic 'tell us' (dire + ci). s’a la man destra giace alcuna foce is an older compressed form (s’ a = se a) meaning 'whether on the right hand there lies any outlet' — s’ (se) introduces the indirect question, a la man destra = 'on the right hand/side,' giace = 'lies/is situated,' alcuna foce = 'any mouth/outlet.' onde introduces the purpose/means clause 'from which' (onde noi amendue possiamo uscirci): noi = 'we,' amendue = 'both,' possiamo = 'we can,' uscirci = pronominal 'get out/escape (for us).'
segments.inferno-23-g148: sanza = 'without.' costrigner is the infinitive meaning 'to compel/force,' and de li angeli neri = 'the black angels' (de + article li). che vegn(an) d’esto fondo a dipartirci is a relative clause: che = 'who,' vegn(an) = 'come' (present), d’esto fondo = 'from this bottom/depth,' a dipartirci = 'to part/separate us' (dipartirci = cause us to be separated). The whole phrase asks that they be allowed to exit without forcing those angels to separate them.
segments.inferno-23-g149: Rispuose is the past historic 'he answered/replied.' adunque is a connective adverb ('therefore/then') introducing the reply.
segments.inferno-23-g150: Più che tu non speri introduces a temporal/comparative sense 'sooner/more quickly than you expect.' s’appressa (si appressa) is the reflexive present 'approaches/is coming near'; un sasso 'a stone/boulder' is the subject of the following relative clause che da la gran cerchia si move e varca tutt’i vallon feri — 'which from the great circle moves itself and crosses all the fierce ravines.' The verbs are in the narrative present (poetic usage) describing motion and crossing.
segments.inferno-23-g151: salvo che = 'except that.' ’n questo (poetic contraction for 'in questo') è rotto = 'is broken here' (è + past participle rotto). e nol coperchia = 'and does not cover it' (nol = non + lo). The clause qualifies the previous statement by noting a local gap where the rock won't cover/block the place.
segments.inferno-23-g152: montar potrete su uses the archaic infinitive + future inversion: literal order 'to climb you will be able up,' meaning 'you will be able to climb up.' per la ruina = 'by/over the ruins/rubble' indicates the route.
segments.inferno-23-g153: che is the relative 'which' and giace 'lies/is situated.' in costa = 'on the slope' and nel fondo soperchia means it overhangs or over-tops the bottom; soperchia here conveys the sense of projecting over/covering the depth below.
segments.inferno-23-g154: Lo duca = 'the leader/duke' with the archaic article lo. stette is the past tense 'stayed/paused,' un poco 'a little,' and a testa china is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'with his head bowed' (testa = head, china = bowed).
segments.inferno-23-g155: poi = 'then/afterwards.' disse is simple past 'he said,' introducing the reported speech that follows.
segments.inferno-23-g156: Mal contava la bisogna: mal (adverb) modifies contava (past 'he reckoned/estimated'), so 'he judged/estimated the need badly' or 'he badly measured what was needed.' colui che i peccator di qua uncina is a relative clause identifying 'he' — 'the one who hooks/snags the sinners on this side' (uncina = hooks/snare, used figuratively for ensnaring or punishing sinners on this side).
segments.inferno-23-g122: This is the closing quotation mark and punctuation (»;). It marks the end of the prior speech and functions as a pause before the narrator continues; there are no lexical items to parse here.
segments.inferno-23-g123: 'ma' = 'but' (introducing a contrast); 'più non dissi' is a negative past statement, with 'più' here meaning 'any longer' and 'dissi' the passato remoto (I said). Word order is subjectless: the verb carries the first-person sense ('I said no more').
segments.inferno-23-g124: The clause begins with 'ch’' (che = 'that/when') introducing the circumstance that follows. 'a l’occhio' = 'to the eye' (literally 'to the eye', i.e. 'into my sight'); 'mi' is an indirect object meaning 'to me'; 'corse' is passato remoto of 'correre' used figuratively ('ran/hurried') meaning 'came swiftly into view'. The indefinite 'un' follows (anticipating the noun phrase in the next segment).
segments.inferno-23-g125: 'crucifisso' is a past participle used adjectivally/nominatively ('one crucified'); 'in terra' literally 'in the earth' = 'to/into the ground' (here: fixed into the ground); 'con tre pali' is an instrumental phrase 'with three stakes/poles' specifying how he is fixed. Word order places the participle and prepositional phrases after the anticipatory 'un' from the previous segment.
segments.inferno-23-g126: 'Quando' = 'when'; 'mi' is direct object 'me'; 'vide' is passato remoto of 'vedere' ('he saw me'). The clause sets the temporal frame for the reaction that follows.
segments.inferno-23-g127: 'tutto' here functions adverbially ('entirely/completely'); 'si distorse' is the reflexive passato remoto of 'distorgere/distorsi' meaning 'he twisted/contorted himself' (a bodily or facial reaction). The reflexive 'si' marks the action as happening to the subject.
segments.inferno-23-g128: 'soffiando' is a present participle ('blowing'/'puffing') that describes an action simultaneous to the previous verb; 'ne la barba' is an archaic locative use of 'ne' + article = 'in his beard'; 'con sospiri' ('with sighs') gives the manner. The participle clause modifies the previous clause.
segments.inferno-23-g129: 'e' = 'and'; '’l' is the elided article 'il' before a vowel-marking apostrophe; 'frate' = 'friar/brother' (religious title) and 'Catalan' is his identifier. Together they name the speaker who follows.
segments.inferno-23-g130: 'ch’' = 'che' ('who/which'); 'a ciò' = 'of/about this' or 'to this' (referring to the preceding action); 's’accorse' is 'si accorse' (passato remoto of 'accorgersi') meaning 'he noticed' or 'became aware'. The relative clause identifies Catalan as the one who noticed.
segments.inferno-23-g131: 'mi' = 'to me' (indirect object); 'disse' is passato remoto of 'dire' ('said/told'). This is the typical formula introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-23-g132: Opening of the quoted speech: 'Quel' = 'that' (demonstrative); 'confitto' is a participle meaning 'fixed/fastened/pierced' (here describing one crucified or impaled); 'che tu miri' is a relative clause 'whom you behold' with 'tu miri' in the present indicative ('you look at/you see').
segments.inferno-23-g133: 'consigliò' is passato remoto 'advised/counselled'; the subject 'i Farisei' (the Pharisees) follows the verb (inversion common in poetry). 'che convenia' introduces a subordinate clause with 'convenia' as an impersonal past ('it was fitting/necessary'); 'porre' is the infinitive 'to place/put'; 'un uom' = 'a man'; 'per lo popolo' = 'for the people'; 'a’ martìri' (a + martìri) = 'to/for martyrdom' or 'to torments' — the whole subordinate clause reports the Pharisees' counsel.
segments.inferno-23-g134: 'Attraversato' is a past participle placed before the copula for poetic effect; here it means 'pierced through' / 'impaled'. 'è' is the present copula 'is' — together they form a stative/passive description ('is impaled').
segments.inferno-23-g135: 'nudo' is an adjective 'naked' (postpositive here) modifying the understood subject; its placement after the participle is stylistic and descriptive.
segments.inferno-23-g136: 'ne la via' is an archaic locative phrase: 'ne' + article = 'in the', so 'in the road' / 'on the highway'; the prepositional phrase gives the place where the state obtains.
segments.inferno-23-g137: 'come tu vedi' is a parenthetical 'as you see' with 'come' = 'as', 'tu' subject and 'vedi' present indicative 'you see'. It points the reader back to the visible scene.
segments.inferno-23-g138: 'ed è mestier' is an impersonal expression meaning 'and it is necessary'; 'ch’' introduces a subjunctive clause. The construction contains a pleonastic or resumptive 'el' (here functioning as a correlative/pleonastic pronoun) and 'senta' is present subjunctive of 'sentire' ('feel/experience' or 'be aware of'). 'qualunque passa' ('whichever/ whoever passes') functions as the person who must perform the action. Overall: 'it is necessary that whoever passes should feel him.'
segments.inferno-23-g139: 'come' here = 'how' (introducing the manner/degree) and 'pesa' (present indicative of 'pesare') means 'is heavy' or 'weighs'; together they mean 'how heavy he is' or 'how it weighs on him'.
segments.inferno-23-g140: 'pria' is an adverb meaning 'before' or 'first'; it modifies the clause, indicating that the feeling/assessment should occur beforehand or first.
segments.inferno-23-g141: 'E' = 'and'; 'a tal modo' = 'in such a way'; 'il socero' = 'my father-in-law' (subject); 'si stenta' is present reflexive 'he struggles/suffers' (si + verb indicates the subject undergoes hardship); 'in questa fossa' = 'in this pit/ditch' is the locative complement. The clause describes ongoing suffering.
segments.inferno-23-g142: The second coordinated clause: 'e li altri' = 'and the others'; 'dal concilio' = 'from the council' (dal = da + il); 'che fu per li Giudei' = 'which was for the Jews' with 'fu' passato remoto of 'essere' ('was'); 'mala sementa' = 'bad seed' (figurative: ill-born/evil stock). The clause condemns the council as harmful and groups the others with the sufferer.

sentences.inferno-23-s21: And one who heard the harsh word cried from behind us: "Hold your feet, you who run so through the dark air!"
sentences.inferno-23-s22: Perhaps you will have from me what you ask.
